title: Fix resuming after `ds.set_epoch(new_epoch)`
body:
close https://github.com/huggingface/datasets/issues/7447

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

step_plan.summary: Fix load_state_dict in _BaseExamplesIterable so loading a partial state does not overwrite existing in-memory subtrees with None; this preserves RNG/epoch state set by ds.set_epoch(new_epoch) and restores correct resume behavior for iterable datasets.

steps.operation: Make load_state_dict preserve existing subtrees when new_state is None (merge semantics)
steps.reason: The recursive helper in _BaseExamplesIterable.load_state_dict currently returns new_state unconditionally, which causes existing in-memory subtrees (including RNG/epoch state set by ds.set_epoch) to be overwritten with None when new_state has None for that subtree. Changing the fallback so that a None new_state preserves the existing state subtree turns load_state_dict into a merge operation and prevents unintentional clobbering of RNG/epoch state.
steps.side_effects: Behavioral: Calls to load_state_dict that include None in some subtrees will no longer wipe the corresponding existing in-memory subtrees; instead those subtrees will be preserved. This fixes resuming after ds.set_epoch(new_epoch) for all iterable dataset variants that inherit from _BaseExamplesIterable (shuffle/cycle/etc.). Tests: Existing tests that relied on the old behavior of replacing subtrees with None may need to be updated; add (or update) a unit test that verifies resuming after set_epoch(new_epoch) (advance dataset, save state, set_epoch, load saved state, ensure RNG/epoch-driven behavior is preserved). Dependents/API: No public API change; change is internal to iterable dataset state handling, but it changes semantics of load_state_dict (from overwrite-with-None to preserve-on-None). Backwards compatibility: likely safe and desirable, but audit callers that intentionally expected None-to-clobber behavior. Performance: negligible extra branching in the recursive helper.
